us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed, Iran Conflict Escalates as Deadline Looms
us-politics.body:
American military aircraft have been shot down by enemy fire for the first time in over 20 years, as the US-Iran conflict intensifies. Trump has warned Tehran that "time is running out" before "all hell" follows, with Senator Graham urging Iran to "choose wisely." The losses mark a serious battlefield setback.

Critics are divided on what comes next. Rep. Moulton declared Iran is winning the war, while former National Security Advisor Bolton suggested Trump may be in "panic mode." Opinion writers argue the US can still prevail through attrition without ground troops, though others question what "finishing the job" would actually mean in practice.

Secretary Rubio revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani living in the US, who were subsequently arrested by ICE - a move framed by the administration as a national security measure.

sources.title: TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down
Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed
Bolton: Trump likely in ‘panic mode’ after Iran attacks US fighter jets

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Proposes Defense Surge, Deep Domestic Cuts
us-politics.body:
Trump has unveiled a budget proposal seeking $1.5 trillion in defense spending while cutting sharply across domestic programs. The plan includes a $152 million request to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison. Supporters argue the shift reflects necessary priorities in a period of active military conflict.

Critics warn the cuts will hit ordinary Americans hardest at a time when recession fears are growing. One analysis describes the US as heading toward what could be its worst economic downturn yet, with the middle class already under sustained pressure. Former Republican strategist Kayleigh McEnany has flagged the political risk, warning that midterm elections will be "hard" for Republicans if economic conditions deteriorate.

sources.title: Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block
America is heading for a recession — and it may be the worst yet
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request

us-politics.headline: Courts and Congress Push Back on Trump Domestic Agenda
us-politics.body:
A federal judge has blocked Trump's effort to require colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions, the latest in a string of judicial checks on administration policy. Separately, Trump filed an emergency appeal to continue construction of a White House ballroom, a project that has drawn legal challenges over historic preservation rules.

On Capitol Hill, bipartisan senators Roger Wicker and Jeanne Shaheen raised concerns about a planned US visit by Russian lawmakers. Meanwhile, a California pipeline order from the White House has drawn criticism from state sovereignty advocates, who warn other states could face similar federal intervention. Senator Blumenthal dismissed Trump's college sports reform order as "theater, not a fix."

sources.title: Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom
Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
Trump crossed an oily line of state sovereignty in California — your state is next
Blumenthal: Trump’s order to reform college sports ‘is theater, not a fix’
Wicker, Shaheen raise concerns about US visit by Russian lawmakers

us-politics.headline: Global Standing Slips as China Tops US in Approval Ratings
us-politics.body:
A new Gallup poll shows China has surpassed the United States in global approval ratings, a shift that reflects growing international skepticism toward American leadership. The finding comes as the US is engaged in active military conflict with Iran and faces criticism abroad over trade and foreign policy moves.

At home, the political landscape is shifting ahead of the 2026 midterms. A poll shows a narrow majority of Virginians support redistricting to favor Democrats. In California, Rep. Eric Swalwell's clash with the FBI is drawing attention to his gubernatorial bid, while a child of Chinese illegal immigrants has been charged with planting an explosive at a US military base - a case Fox News has highlighted amid broader immigration and national security debates.

sources.title: China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup
Swalwell-FBI clash draws attention to Trump foe’s California governor bid
Narrow majority in Virginia backs redistricting to favor Democrats: Poll
Child of Chinese illegal immigrants charged with planting explosive at US military base

au-politics.headline: Albanese's Gambling Ad Gamble Divides Labor and Voters
au-politics.body:
Anthony Albanese is under pressure over his handling of gambling advertising reform, with critics arguing Labor has repeatedly delayed meaningful action to protect relationships with the racing and sports industries. The Murphy Report recommended sweeping restrictions, but the government's response has been cautious and drawn accusations of prioritising political survival over public health.

With an election approaching, the decision carries real risk in both directions. Acting too softly alienates progressive voters and health advocates. Acting too boldly risks backlash from powerful industry lobbies and regional communities where racing is economically significant. Neither side of the debate is letting Albanese off lightly.

sources.title: Trash or treasure? Albanese’s big gamble

au-politics.headline: Stagflation Bites as Young Australians Skip Work Over Fuel
au-politics.body:
A leading economist has declared stagflation is already underway in Australia, combining stalled growth with persistent inflation. The warning comes as young workers report they cannot afford to drive to their jobs, with fuel costs eating into wages to the point where working becomes financially irrational for some.

The squeeze is sharpest for people in outer suburbs and regional areas with no viable public transport alternative. Economists disagree on the right policy response - cutting rates risks inflaming inflation further, while holding them steady deepens the cost-of-living crunch. The government faces pressure to act, but the tools available carry their own risks.

sources.title: Stagflation 'is happening' says a leading economist. What can be done about it?
'Doesn't make any sense': Young Aussies can't justify fuel costs to get to work

au-politics.headline: Iran Conflict Escalates as Children Recruited for Ground War
au-politics.body:
The conflict involving Iran is intensifying, with reports that children as young as 12 are being recruited into armed roles as preparations for a potential ground invasion accelerate. A US pilot is also missing, prompting a joint search operation while Donald Trump has issued stark warnings of severe consequences if a diplomatic deal is not reached.

The situation is fast-moving and the stakes are high. Recruitment of minors into armed conflict would constitute a serious breach of international law. Analysts are divided on whether Trump's pressure tactics will force a negotiated outcome or push the region closer to broader war.

sources.title: Live: US and Iran race to find missing pilot as Trump threatens 'Hell' if deal not reached
Children as young as 12 recruited to join Iran war

au-politics.headline: Australian Universities Face Reputation Crisis Over Commercialisation
au-politics.body:
Australia's university sector is at a crossroads, with critics arguing that decades of over-reliance on international student revenue has hollowed out academic standards and damaged the country's reputation abroad. The term 'enshitification' - borrowed from tech criticism - is being applied to a system where financial pressures have steadily eroded educational quality.

Universities dispute the framing but acknowledge the funding model is unsustainable. Federal policy has long encouraged international enrolments as a revenue substitute for reduced public funding. The debate now centres on whether reform should come through tighter regulation, restored government investment, or both.

sources.title: The 'enshitification' of the system has left Australia's unis at a crossroad

world.headline: US-Israeli strikes escalate as Trump gives Iran 48-hour ultimatum
world.body:
The United States and Israel have intensified strikes on Iranian infrastructure, hitting universities in Tehran and damaging a bridge, as the conflict enters a sharper phase. A projectile struck near Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one person and prompting the UN's nuclear watchdog to voice deep concern. Iran condemned the university strikes, saying the US and Israel "belong in the Stone Age."

A US fighter jet was shot down over Iran, and a search is underway for the missing crew member under what rescuers describe as harrowing conditions. Trump has given Iran 48 hours to reach a deal. The US also arrested relatives of slain general Qasem Soleimani and stripped his niece of US residency, signalling pressure beyond the battlefield.

sources.title: US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Top diplomat Marco Rubio strips Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
New video reveals moment US-Israeli munitions impact Iranian bridge
Trump says Iran has 48 hours to make deal as search for US pilot continues
UN watchdog voices 'deep concern' as Iran reports new attacks on nuclear plant
Iran says US, Israel belong in Stone Age after Tehran university strike
Projectile hits near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one: IAEA
What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member

world.headline: Israel expands strikes to Lebanon as Gaza bombardment continues
world.body:
Israeli forces struck a building in Lebanon's Tyre and damaged a hospital there, as a ground invasion advances further into Lebanese territory. A drone strike in central Gaza engulfed a vehicle in flames, part of ongoing bombardment across the territory. Displaced Lebanese civilians describe lives turned upside down with little prospect of return.

Italian Prime Minister Meloni met the Qatari emir to discuss energy security amid the widening regional war, reflecting growing European anxiety about supply disruptions. Iranian projectile debris also struck vehicles inside Israel, showing the conflict is drawing retaliatory fire in both directions.

sources.title: Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza
Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Vehicles burn in Israel after being hit with Iranian projectile debris
Israeli strikes damage hospital in Lebanon’s Tyre; ground invasion advances

world.headline: Russia kills at least 15 in Ukraine as Zelenskyy meets Erdogan
world.body:
Russian attacks on Ukraine killed at least 15 people, including five in a strike on a market. President Zelenskyy met Turkish President Erdogan, likely seeking diplomatic leverage as the war grinds on with no ceasefire in sight.

The market strike underlines Russia's continued targeting of civilian areas despite international pressure.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
At least 15 killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine as Zelenskyy meets Erdogan

world.headline: UK charity attack suspects held; Cameroon's ageing ruler gets first deputy
world.body:
Three suspects have been remanded in UK custody following an attack on a Jewish charity, with a court ordering they remain detained as the investigation continues. The case has heightened tensions around antisemitic violence in Britain.

In a rare political shift, Cameroon's Paul Biya, the world's oldest head of state at 92, has appointed a deputy for the first time in his 43-year rule, fuelling speculation about succession in one of Africa's longest-running authoritarian governments.

sources.title: World's oldest leader to get a deputy for first time in 43-year rule
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack

ai.headline: Anthropic Locks OpenClaw Behind Paywall, Angering Claude Users
ai.body:
Anthropic has moved to effectively block OpenClaw from Claude Code by requiring subscribers to pay extra for the integration, a change users are calling a quiet ban. The Verge and TechCrunch both confirmed the policy shift, which hit without much warning and immediately drew backlash on Hacker News.

The timing is awkward. Anthropic is riding high in private markets, with investors circling and valuations climbing - but moves like this risk souring the developer community that made Claude Code a standout product. Separately, hackers are now distributing a Claude Code leak bundled with malware, adding a security headache to an already messy week for the company.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw

ai.headline: Claude Finds 23-Year-Old Linux Bug, Raises AI Coding Stakes
ai.body:
Claude Code uncovered a Linux vulnerability that had sat undetected for 23 years, a result that will sharpen debates about AI's role in security research. The find was documented by Michael Lynch, who walked through how the model identified the flaw during routine code analysis.

Researchers are also making progress on the theory side. A new paper shows that embarrassingly simple self-distillation techniques meaningfully improve code generation quality, suggesting the ceiling for current models is higher than assumed. A separate Anthropic study examined how emotion concepts function inside large language models, adding to growing interest in what is actually happening inside these systems.

sources.title: Embarrassingly simple self-distillation improves code generation
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years

ai.headline: AI Fakes Threaten Folk Musicians as Authenticity Becomes a Selling Point
ai.body:
Folk musician Murphy Campbell became a target for AI-generated fakes and a copyright troll simultaneously, a case The Verge uses to illustrate how independent artists are caught between two converging threats. AI tools can now clone a niche artist's voice cheaply enough to flood streaming platforms, while bad-faith copyright claims pile on the legal pressure.

The backlash is spawning a counter-movement. Creators are now actively marketing human-made work as a premium, with logos and certifications emerging to signal AI-free content. Whether audiences will pay more for that guarantee remains an open question, but the market signal is clear.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Apple Greenlights Nvidia eGPU Driver for Arm Macs
ai.body:
Apple has approved a driver allowing Nvidia external GPUs to work with Arm-based Macs, a notable thaw given the companies' long-running GPU estrangement. The move gives Mac users a practical path to serious external graphics without waiting for Apple Silicon to close every performance gap.

For developers, the timing aligns with growing demand for local GPU compute. A new service called sllm is pitching shared GPU node access with unlimited tokens, targeting teams that need inference capacity without the cost of dedicated hardware. The GPU-as-infrastructure market is clearly heating up from multiple directions.

sources.title: Apple approves driver that lets Nvidia eGPUs work with Arm Macs
Show HN: sllm – Split a GPU node with other developers, unlimited tokens

btc.headline: Bitcoin Draws Institutional Backing as Market Liquidity Thins
btc.body:
A former UK Chancellor has publicly endorsed Bitcoin as a hedge against failing financial systems, adding political weight to a growing institutional narrative. Research from Mercado Bitcoin reinforces the case, finding that Bitcoin consistently outperforms gold and equities in the aftermath of global shocks.

Beneath the bullish framing, the market structure tells a more cautious story. Five separate data sources point to thinning liquidity inside the Bitcoin order book, a sign that depth is eroding even as prices hold. Large traders absorbed heavy losses averaging $337 million per day through Q1 2026, suggesting the rally has been punishing for leveraged positions on both sides.

Shorts are not safe either. Around $2.5 billion in short positions face liquidation if Bitcoin pushes to $72,000, putting bears in a precarious spot.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems
Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside
Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Face Legal Walls From Nevada to Asia
btc.body:
Prediction markets are running into hard regulatory resistance on multiple fronts. A Nevada judge extended the ban on Kalshi, rejecting the platform's argument that its event contracts fall outside gambling law. The ruling signals that US courts are not ready to accept the event-contract framing that prediction platforms have leaned on.

The pressure is not limited to the US. Platforms testing prediction markets across strict Asian jurisdictions are hitting similar walls, with regulators treating the products as unlicensed gambling regardless of how they are structured. Polymarket added to the sector's reputational headaches by pulling a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash, a reminder that legal exposure and ethical optics are now equally live risks for the industry.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Node Debate and Solana's Quantum Security Dilemma
btc.body:
Jimmy Song is pushing for a more conservative Bitcoin node client, arguing that the network's resilience depends on software that resists feature creep and prioritizes stability over experimentation. The proposal cuts against a culture that often rewards technical ambition, but Song frames caution as a security feature in itself.

Solana faces a starker version of the same tension. Analysis of the network's quantum-threat readiness reveals a direct tradeoff - hardening against future quantum attacks requires architectural changes that would meaningfully slow transaction throughput. Speed is Solana's core selling point, making the security upgrade politically and technically difficult. Both debates reflect a broader maturation across crypto infrastructure, where the cost of getting foundational decisions wrong is becoming harder to ignore.

sources.title: Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client
Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed

btc.headline: 24/7 Stock Trading and Crypto Treasuries Reshape Market Norms
btc.body:
The push toward round-the-clock stock trading is gaining momentum, with proponents arguing it will eliminate the after-hours price gaps that sophisticated players have long exploited. For crypto traders already accustomed to markets that never close, the shift levels a playing field that has historically favored institutional desks with overnight access.

Separately, a CoinDesk opinion piece argues that corporate digital asset treasuries can no longer justify passive Bitcoin holdings as a strategy. As the asset class matures, the expectation is shifting toward treasuries that generate yield or serve a clear operational purpose. Holding Bitcoin on a balance sheet as a store of value alone is increasingly seen as insufficient justification for the associated risk and volatility.

sources.title: Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep
Traders are the big winners as 24/7 stocks will finally end the after-hours price 'manipulation'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust shell filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that balances umami with subtle sweetness.
food.tip: Rinse sliced leeks in cold water and dry them thoroughly before caramelising — trapped moisture is what makes them steam instead of colour.

exercise.name: The Saturday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and back to moderate — training your body to find and hold different effort gears rather than just grinding at one pace.

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous systems of crayfish, by medical devices for balance disorders, and in image processing.
learn.why: It quietly upends the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you understand it you start spotting its fingerprints in biology, economics, and engineering in ways that feel almost conspiratorial.
